coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots/copper nanoclusters/metal ions assemble for a ratiometric ﬂuorescence determination of an anthrax biomarker through ligand displacement strategy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Dou, Huashuo
Lu, Yuexiang
Zhang, Xiwen
Wang, Jiaxi
Liu, Yueying
coredata.dc:description: Exploring a novel strategy for the sensitive and rapid detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an anthrax spore biomarker is helpful to prevent anthrax disease and biological terrorist attack. Herein, a nanocomposite integrating carbon quantum dots with copper nanoclusters/Al3+-Eu3+ (CDs-CAA/Eu3+) is constructed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for DPA detection, whose sensing mechanism depends on the ligand displacement rather than the antenna effect of ligand and rare earth ions, avoiding excessive resource consumption and reliance on short excitation wavelengths. Remarkably, the orange-red fluorescence of copper nanoclusters/Al3+ (CAA) is enhanced about 280-fold compared to dispersed copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Oppositely, CAA is significantly quenched after introducing Eu3+ owing to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). CAA can be replaced with DPA because of the formation of Eu3+-DPA complexes. So, the fluorescence of CAA is restored upon exposure of DPA, which is attributed to the detachment of Eu3+ from CAA. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CDs) as an internal reference is hardly affected by DPA. Compared to a single emission of CAA/Eu3+, this ratiometric method offers wider linear range (0.3–550 μM), better visualization, and lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 μM. Moreover, a strategy combining with smartphone is established for visualization detection of DPA in the range of 8.0–400 μM with LOD of 5.7 μM, which is lower than DPA released by infectious spores (60 µM). This approach is successfully employed to determine DPA in various real samples and provides significant potential for rapidly monitoring DPA in on-site application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ligand displacement mechanism
Ratiometric ﬂuorescence probe
Dual-emission CDs-CAA/Eu3+
2,6-dipicolinic acid
Visual detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CuNCs, CAA, and CAA/Eu3+
Preparation of the visual sensor for DPA assay
Ligand displacement mechanism for DPA detection
Parameters optimization for the detection of DPA

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and Characterization of CuNCs, CAA, and CAA/Eu3+. (a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CAA/Eu3+. TEM images: (b) CuNCs. (c) CAA. (d) CAA/Eu3+. (e) Size distribution by DLS. (f) FTIR spectra. (g) Zeta potential. (h) High-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu 2p of CuNCs. (i) Fluorescence emission spectra. Inset: photographs under 365 nm UV light. (j) Fluorescence decay curves.

(a) Schematic illustration of the preparation of CDs-CAA/Eu3+. (b) TEM images of CDs. (c) Size distribution of CDs, CAA/Eu3+, and CDs, CAA/Eu3+ by DLS. (d) Normalized, UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra (Inset: photograph under the 365 nm lamp) of CDs. Characterization of CDs-CAA/Eu3+: (e) TEM images. (f) Normalized UV–vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra (Inset: photograph under the 365 nm lamp) of CDs-CAA/Eu3+.

(a) Schematic illustration of the detection of DPA by CAA/Eu3+. Characterization of CAA, CAA/Eu3+, and CAA/Eu3+-DPA: (b) Normalized fluorescence emission. (c) Zeta potential. (d) Size distribution. (e) Schematic illustration of the detection of DPA by the ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+. Characterization of CDs-CAA, CDs-CAA/Eu3+ and CDs-CAA/Eu3+-DPA: (f) Normalized fluorescence emission. (g) Zeta potential. (h) Size distribution.

Optimized key parameters: (a) Effect of metal cations on the CuNCs; (b) Effect of Al3+ concentration on CuNCs; (c) Effect of metal cations on CAA; (d) Effect of Eu3+ concentration on CAA; (e) Visualization photographs of various systems with different ratios of CDs to CAA/Eu3+ to detect DPA (0–1.0 mM) under UV 365 lamp. (I) 1:8, (II) 1:10; (III) 1:12, (IV) 1:14; (f) reaction time of different systems including CAA, CAA/Eu3+, CAA/Eu3+-DPA, CDs-CAA/Eu3+, and CDs-CAA/Eu3+-DPA.

A single fluorescence probe of CAA/Eu3+ for DPA detection: (a) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra (Inset: photographs under the 365 nm lamp); (b) Calibration plot with a single emission peak at 610 nm. Ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ for DPA detection: (c) Three-dimensional fluorescence spectra, (d) CIE chromaticity diagram (Inset, photographs under 365 nm lamp), (e) Calibration plot with the ratio value of I464/I610. (f) Selectivity experiments of the CDs-CAA/Eu3+ sensing system in the presence of possible interfering substances or DPA.

(a) Schematic diagram of the CDs-CAA/Eu3+ fluorescent probe with Smartphone readout for point-of-care testing of DPA. (b) Photographs of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ with different DPA concentrations under the 365 nm UV lamp. (c) Relationship between the colorimetric intensity (B/R) value and DPA concentration (8.0–400 µM).
Schematic diagram of synthesis route of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ and ratiometric ﬂuorescence sensing of DPA based on ligand displacement mechanism.
Determination of DPA in real samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots/copper nanoclusters/metal ions assemble for a ratiometric ﬂuorescence determination of an anthrax biomarker through ligand displacement strategy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Capital Normal University, Beijing, 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Collaborative Innovation Center of Advanced Nuclear Energy Technology, Institute of Nuclear and New Energy Technology, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Exploring a novel strategy for the sensitive and rapid detection of 2,6-dipicolinic acid (DPA) as an anthrax spore biomarker is helpful to prevent anthrax disease and biological terrorist attack. Herein, a nanocomposite integrating carbon quantum dots with copper nanoclusters/Al3+-Eu3+ (CDs-CAA/Eu3+) is constructed as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for DPA detection, whose sensing mechanism depends on the ligand displacement rather than the antenna effect of ligand and rare earth ions, avoiding excessive resource consumption and reliance on short excitation wavelengths. Remarkably, the orange-red fluorescence of copper nanoclusters/Al3+ (CAA) is enhanced about 280-fold compared to dispersed copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) due to aggregation-induced emission (AIE). Oppositely, CAA is significantly quenched after introducing Eu3+ owing to aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ). CAA can be replaced with DPA because of the formation of Eu3+-DPA complexes. So, the fluorescence of CAA is restored upon exposure of DPA, which is attributed to the detachment of Eu3+ from CAA. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CDs) as an internal reference is hardly affected by DPA. Compared to a single emission of CAA/Eu3+, this ratiometric method offers wider linear range (0.3–550 μM), better visualization, and lower limit of detection (LOD) of 0.1 μM. Moreover, a strategy combining with smartphone is established for visualization detection of DPA in the range of 8.0–400 μM with LOD of 5.7 μM, which is lower than DPA released by infectious spores (60 µM). This approach is successfully employed to determine DPA in various real samples and provides significant potential for rapidly monitoring DPA in on-site application.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Integrating carbon quantum dots with copper nanoclusters/Al3+-Eu3+ nanocomposites as a ratiometric fluorescence probe is constructed to detect DPA as a unique biomarker for anthrax spores, whose sensing mechanism depends on the ligand displacement rather than the antenna effect of ligand and rare earth ions. Moreover, a strategy combining with smartphone is established for visualization detection of DPA.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacillus anthracis (B. anthracis) is a gram-positive, spore-forming, and rod-shaped bacterium and can easily cause the highly infectious anthrax as a zoonotic disease [1,2]. Anthracis spores can survive for extended periods without nutrients and exhibit high resistance to extreme environments, such as high temperature, ultraviolet radiation, freezing, strong acids/bases, and chemical disinfection [3]. Even when the bacteria are killed, the spores can still germinate under favorable conditions [4,5]. Anthrax spores dispersed by aerosol can pollute the air, water, soil, and food in large areas at the same time [6]. After inhaling more than 104 anthrax spores, the rate of mortality reaches up to almost 100 % unless timely and effective treatment within 24–48 h [7–9]. Accordingly, Anthrax spores as a potential lethal biological warfare agent attract considerable concern all over the world especially after the anthrax attacks in 2001 [10,11]. Therefore, the rapid and sensitive detection of anthrax spores is very crucial to avoid anthrax infection, protect food security, and prevent potential bioterrorism [12]. It is worth noting that 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid (also known as dipicolinic acid, DPA) mainly exists in the protective layer of spores and can be released from anthrax spores by germination, hydrolysis, and heating, accounting for 5–15 % of their dry mass [13]. DPA cannot be found in other common spores (such as pollen and mold) or occurring environmental materials, making it a unique and excellent biomarker for anthrax [14,15]. The dormant spores can germinate under right conditions and then ultimately convert back into a growing cell within 30 min. Thus, the rapid and sensitive DPA assay methods will be valuable for anthrax risk monitoring and treatment [16].

Among the various reported methods for DPA detection, ﬂuorescence techniques have gained considerable interest due to their high sensitivity, fast response, and simplicity [6]. Compared to the traditional methods depending on the enhancement or weakening of a single ﬂuorescence emission, the ratiometric fluorescent approach can employ the ratio of two fluorescence intensities to determine DPA, which can effectively eliminate interferences from background ﬂuorescence, instrument ﬂuctuation, and operational variations through the self-calibration process [17,18]. Concurrently, ratiometric method can improve the accuracy and sensitivity of the detection and often achieve profuse color evolution rather than only brightness change of a single wavelength emission with the assistance of UV lamps, which realizes the visual detection of DPA for naked-eye [19]. Various lanthanide-functionalized materials (particularly Eu3+ or Tb3+) have dominated to construct a ratiometric sensor for the detection of DPA owing to the formation of the stable Eu3+/Tb3+-DPA complexes [20]. The sensing mechanism of most of ratiometric approaches mainly depend on “antenna eﬀect” or sensitization of lanthanide ions. [21]. DPA acting as “antenna” coordinates with Eu3+/Tb3+ of the probe, and then transfers the absorbed energy to lanthanide ions, leading to characteristic ﬂuorescence emission of the sensitized Eu3+/Tb3+. Unfortunately, excessive amount of lanthanide resource is consumed during these ratiometric probes synthesis, resulting in an inefficient utilization of lanthanide resource, time-consuming, and laborious post process [22]. Additionally, emission spectra are obtained to require short excitation wavelengths (250–300 nm) [23,24]. To address this problem, the ligand displacement mechanism is developed to monitor DPA [25]. The fixed content of Eu3+/Tb3+ can non-covalently bind to the fluorescence probe surface accompanying with emission quenching, and then DPA competes with the functional probe for complexing with Eu3+/Tb3+, resulting in lanthanide ions detachment from the surface of probe. Finally, the fluorescence emission of the probe restores. This approach is efficient to avoid excessive resource consumption [26,27]. At the same time, the longer excitation wavelength depends on the characteristic fluorescent emission of the probe rather than the spectroscopic properties of lanthanides. However, few ratiometric ﬂuorescence probes are capable of identifying DPA base on the ligand displacement strategy [6,28]. Cadmium sulfide quantum dots and rhodamine 6 G as a reference signal encapsulated into zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 (ZIF-8) nanocrystals (CdS QDs/R6G@ZIF-8) are designed as a ratiometric probe, which can be quenched due to the coordination reaction of QDs and Eu3+. The formation of the more stable Eu3+-DPA complexes can be separated from QDs/R6G@ZIF-8 in concomitant with the system fluorescence recovery [6]. Similarly, gold nanoclusters as a response signal and graphene quantum dots as an internal reference (AuNCs/GQDs) embedded into ZIF-8 as ratiometric probe is unitized for the detection of DPA [28]. Therefore, the development of novel ratiometric fluorescent sensors is urgently required for DPA detection based on the ligand displacement strategy.

Metal nanoclusters including gold, silver, and copper nanoclusters have garnered significant attention due to their inherent advantages, good biocompatibility, easy of synthesis, and large stokes shift [29,30]. Unfortunately, the application of metal nanoclusters in biosensing and imaging is greatly limited due to low fluorescence efficiency [31]. Recent studies have shown that metal nanoclusters have significant AIE properties triggered by some metal ions, which may greatly improve the luminescence properties of nanoclusters and promote their wide application [32–35], because metal ions can coordinate with carboxyl groups of nanoclusters to induce their aggregation. There are a few reporters about metal nanoclusters AIE behavior combined metal ions for the detection of DPA based on the ligand displacement mechanism [26,27,36,37]. However, these systems only offer a single wavelength response in detecting DPA, which is easily influenced by external factors. Compared with gold and silver nanoclusters, copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) have attracted more attention due to their low cost, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and unique optical properties [38]. Additionally, CDs can offer various advantages, such as convenient synthesis, good water-solubility, and tunable emission spectrum [39]. The fluorescence of some CDs as the reference remains unaffected in previous literatures due to the lack of interaction between CDs and the target substances, so they are ideal candidates to construct some ratiometric fluorescence probe [40,41]. Herein, we have designed CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite as a ratiometric fluorescence probe for the determination of DPA based on the ligand displacement mechanism ( Scheme 1). Noticeably, the blue fluorescence of CDs as a reference remains unchanged, while CAA/Eu3+ acts as the sensing unit for DPA. Al3+ can induce glutathione-decorated copper nanoclusters (CuNCs) to rapidly construct CAA nanocomposite by AIE behavior. Surprisingly, due to the ACQ effect, Eu3+ can effectively suppress the orange red emission of CAA. Because of the stronger coordination interaction between DPA and Eu3+, Eu3+ can be removed from the surface of CAA, thus CAA fluorescence can enhance again. So, the constructed ratiometric fluorescence assay for quantitative DPA has been successfully established, demonstrating satisfactory sensitivity and selectivity. Moreover, a strategy combining with smartphone analysis is established for visualization detection of DPA.

Copper dinitrate, aluminum chloride, sodium hydroxide, Europium nitrate hexahydrate, 2,6-pyridinedicarboxylic acid, and adenosine 5’-monophosphate disodium salt are purchased from Beijing Chemical Works (Beijing, China). L-Glutathione reduced is obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). All other reagents are at least analytical grade. The bovine serum was bought from Beijing Yuanheng Shengma Biological Technology Research Institute. Orange juice was purchased from supermarket. Milk was purchased from a local supermarket. The ultrapure water is produced by Milli-Q purification system.

The fluorescence spectra are obtained by a Molecular Devices SpectraMax M2 Multi-Mode Microplate Reader. Fluorescence lifetime is measured on a FLS-1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, U.K.). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra are recorded by ESCALAB Xi+ X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) are performed on a Nicolet 380 spectrometer (Thermo, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images are obtained on a Hitachi (H-7650, 80 kV) transmission electron microscope. The hydrodynamic size and zeta potentials are tested using a Malvern Zetasizer (U.K.).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CuNCs, CAA, and CAA/Eu3+

ce:section.ce:para: CuNCs coated with glutathione (GSH) was synthesized according to the previous study [42]. In detail, 0.0369 g GSH was dispersed into 3.0 mL H2O, and then 3.0 mL Cu(NO3)2 (10 mM) was added with strong stirring at room temperature. Following that, 1.0 mM NaOH was used to adjust the pH to about 7.0. CuNCs were achieved accompanying solution color changes from light yellow to transparent. Finally, CuNCs was stored in the refrigerator for further use.
1.0 mL AlCl3 (10 mM) was added to 2 mL CuNCs with thoroughly shaking to mix well. The white turbidity of the solution proved that CuNCs/Al3+ (CAA) aggregations had been successfully prepared.
45 μL Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (6.0 mM) was added to 135 μL CAA. Ultimately, the solution was incubated 20 min at room temperature.

Based on previous method [43], we synthesize the CDs through hydrothermal method. In brief, 0.1 g of adenosine 5’-monophosphate disodium salt dissolved in 10 mL H2O was transferred to autoclave and heated at 180 ℃ for 8 h. Subsequently, the reactive product was allowed to cool down to the room temperature (25 ℃). Finally, the CDs were obtained.

Briefly, a total of 225 μL CDs diluted by 15 times was mixed with 135 μL CAA, followed by the addition of 45 μL Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (6.0 mM). Ultimately, the probe was obtained and stored at room temperature.

First, 45 μL of DPA with different concentrations of 0–800 μM was added to 405 μL of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ probe solution. The final volume of the mixture was kept at 450 μL. The solutions were transferred to a 96-well plate for fluorescence measurements with excitation at 340 nm.

In the selectivity detection, interfering compounds at 500 μM, or coexisting compounds of interferons and DPA at 1.5 mM was introduced into the CDs-CAA/Eu3+ probe. In the same way, the fluorescence data were measured.

The selectivity was also studied with possible interfering analytes present in blood and urine, e.g., serine (Ser), alanine (Ala), hydroxyproline (Hyp), methionine (Met), Proline (Pro), Zn2+, K+, and Ca2+. Accordingly, to investigate the impact of different interfering substances on DPA detection, 22.5 μL interferons solution (1.5 mM) was added into CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanoprobe solution in the absence and presence of 500 μM DPA. The ﬂuorescence spectra data of solutions were collected using the same instrument and method.

In order to further evaluate the practicality and feasibility of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ probe in practical environments, we added DPA with different concentrations at 40 µM, 250 µM, 500 µM to the real samples including soda water, orange juice, milk, bovine serum, and soil during testing. We preprocessed the actual samples before testing to avoid the influence of interfering substances and other components like biomacromolecules, i.e., proteins. Ultrasonic the soda water for 15 min and set it aside for later use. Soda water was purchased from supermarket. Orange juice diluted 4 times was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. After milk was diluted with 3 % acetic acid, we filtered the solution through a 0.22 µm membrane which was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The bovine serum was diluted 20 times, and then filtered the sample through a 0.22 µm membrane. The soil sample dissolved in water was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and then filtered through a 0.22 µm membrane. The soil was collected from the campus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the visual sensor for DPA assay
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we added 45 μL different concentrations of DPA into the probe solutions. The fluorescent color change can be quickly recognized by the naked eye under the 365 nm UV lamp. Next, we use the color picker feature within the photo editing tool of the smartphone to recognize changes in fluorescent color, and the corresponding RGB values can be quickly read.

CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite as a ratiometric fluorescence probe is established for DPA detection. CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite are synthesized ( Fig. 1a) and act as ﬂuorescence response signals, while CDs are selected as an internal reference signal. Some characterizations are employed to ascertain the successful synthesis of the CAA/Eu3+ . TEM images indicate that the average size of dispersed CuNCs is found to be 1.5 nm (Fig. 1b). Aggregated CuNCs (CAA) can be obtained after adding Al3+ (Fig. 1c). Next, the larger size of aggregation (CAA/Eu3+) is formed upon adding Eu3+ (Fig. 1d). Simultaneously, the DLS measurement indicates that the hydrodynamic diameter of CuNCs, CAA, and CAA/Eu3+ is about 2.6 nm, 212.3 nm, and 327.8 nm (Fig. 1e), respectively. TEM and DLS technique attest that the order of size from small to large is arranged: CuNCs ˂CAA ˂CAA/Eu3+. FTIR spectrum of CuNCs testifies that the peak at 2524 cm−1 assigning to S-H stretching vibration of GSH molecule disappears (Fig. 1f and Fig. S1), suggesting that GSH is anchored on the CuNCs through the Cu-S bonding. A new band at 400–800 cm−1 corresponding to the Al-O vibration in FTIR spectrum of CAA [44]. The symmetric stretching peak of carboxyl group shifts from 1631 cm−1 to 1658 cm−1 and a new band at 709 cm−1 is assigned to the Eu-O vibration, indicating that the carboxyl group is coordinated to the Eu3+ [45]. As shown in Fig. 1g, the zeta potential of CuNCs is −21.7 mV due to the large number of negative charges of -COO- on the surface of CuNCs. After bonding with Al3+, the partial negative charges on the surface are neutralized, resulting in the increase of the zeta potential of CAA to −15.7 mV. Next, the zeta potential of CAA/Eu3+ become opposite positive charges (+13.6 mV). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement shows that that Cu, O, C, N, and S elements existed in CuNCs (Fig. S2). Two peaks with binding energy of 933.7 and 954.7 eV are assigned to 2p3/2 and 2p1/2 electrons of Cu0 and Cu+ species (Fig. 1h). The satellite peaks around 942.0 eV is not present, indicating the absence of Cu(II) in CuNCs [42]. Notably, the absence of surface plasmon resonance band at ∼560 nm indicates the formation of copper nanoclusters rather than copper nanoparticle (Fig. S3). The UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence excitation spectra of CuNCs, CAA, and CAA/Eu3+ have almost no changes (Fig. S3-S4). The faint orange-red fluorescence intensity of CuNCs is obtained at 640 nm under the excitation wavelength of 340 nm (Fig. 1i). The ﬂuorescence intensity of CAA is improved 280-fold compared to dispersed CuNCs due to AIE behavior. The maximum emission wavelength of CAA is blue-shifted to 610 nm. The vibration and rotation of GSH ligands on the surface of the CuNCs are significantly limited by the formation of the Al-O bond, suggesting that the ﬂuorescence of CuNCs is greatly enhanced though reducing the non-radiative decay rate. At the same time, the fluorescence quantum yield of CAA is about 9.14 %, which is enhanced by 23 times compared with that of dispersed CuNCs (∼0.4 %). On the contrary, CAA can be quenched by adding Eu3+ owing to ACQ effects. Their corresponding photographs can be seen under the UV lamp (Inset of Fig. 1i). As shown in Fig. 1j, the average decay lifetime is increases from 4.7 µs for the dispersed CuNCs to 18.8 µs for aggregated CAA. However, the lifetime of CAA/Eu3+ is reduced to 7.5 µs, which is near to the dispersed CuNCs. These results demonstrate that CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite are successfully synthesized by integrating AIE with ACQ behavior.

The blue-emissive CDs can be facilely prepared by hydrothermal process using adenosine 5’-monophosphate disodium salt (AMP) (Fig. S5). The preparation process of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ is provided in Fig. 2a. TEM images display good dispersion of CDs with mean diameter of about 2.3 nm (Fig. 2b) and the DLS results imply that the nanoparticles have an average diameter of 2.7 nm (Fig. 2c). The results of FTIR and XPS further confirm the presence of hydroxyl, amide, amino and phosphate groups (Fig. S6-S7). CDs exhibit an excitation-wavelength independent emission (Fig. S8), implying a narrow size distribution of CDs and highly homogeneous surface structures. The average lifetime of CDs is measured to about be 4.0 ns (Fig. S9) and the absolute quantum yield of 10.78 % for CDs is attained. As displayed in Fig. 2d, two strong absorption bands of the CDs display is located at 260 nm and 342 nm, respectively, which is attributed to the π-π* transition of CC and n-π* transition of CO. The emission peak of CDs can be available at 464 nm with the excitation at 340 nm. CDs solution emits a brightly blue fluorescence under the 365 nm lamp (Inset of Fig. 2d). These data demonstrate that CDs are successfully synthesized. Further, CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite are assembled by the electrostatic interaction between CDs (-27.0 mV) and CAA/Eu3+ (+13.6 mV) (Fig. S10). TEM images certificate that aggregated CDs-CAA/Eu3+ are observed (Fig. 2e). Simultaneously, the diameter of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ (about 346.4 nm) is slightly higher than that of CAA/Eu3+ (about 327.8 nm) by DLS measurement (Fig. 2c). CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposites clearly shows two emission wavelengths of 464 nm and 610 nm with a single excitation wavelength at 340 nm (Fig. 2f). These results indicate that the ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ is successfully synthesized.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ligand displacement mechanism for DPA detection
ce:section.ce:para: A single emission wavelength method is utilized to detect DPA by using ligand displacement mechanism based on CAA/Eu3+ ( Fig. 3a). The emission intensity of CAA/Eu3+ as response signal restores remarkably after adding DPA (Fig. 3b). The fluorescence intensity of CAA shows negligible change after introducing DPA (Fig. S11). The sensing mechanism is explored by using zeta potential, hydrodynamic size, FTIR, and ﬂuorescence lifetime. The zeta potential of the system changes from + 13.6 mV to −9.2 mV after introducing DPA, which is close to the value of CAA (Fig. 3c). The hydrodynamic size of CAA/Eu3+ changes from 327.8 nm to 233.7 nm upon adding DPA, which is very close to that of CAA with a diameter of 213.8 nm (Fig. 3d). The FTIR spectrum of CAA/Eu3+ displays that the peak at 1633 cm−1 (-CO) noticeably shifts to 1651 cm−1 and the peak intensity at 3419 cm−1 (OH stretching) is increased in the presence of DPA. On the contrary, the peak intensity (OH bending) is declined in concomitant with a shift to 613 cm−1. FTIR data confirm that DPA can interact with CAA/Eu3+ (Fig. S12). Further, the average lifetime of CAA/Eu3+ is increased from 7.5 µs to 16.3 µs in the presence of DPA, which almost returns to the decay lifetime of CAA (18.8 µs) (Fig. S13), indicating that Eu3+ is detached from the surface of CAA. Moreover, the fluorescence recovery of the composite system at 610 nm only originates from the CAA at excitation 340 nm without the interference of Eu3+-DPA complexes at 594 nm and 616 nm through the antenna effect at excitation 275 nm (Fig. S14). These data prove that DPA can replace CAA and coordinated with Eu3+. Based on the ligand displacement sensing mechanism, the turn-on fluorescence probe of CAA/Eu3+ is feasible for DPA detection. What’s more, CDs-CAA/Eu3+ as a ratiometric probe is established to detect DPA (Fig. 3e). The single excitation wavelength of 340 nm is chosen according to the overlap of excitation spectra of CDs and CDs-CAA/Eu3+ (Fig. S15). The emission intensity of CAA/Eu3+ at 610 nm restores remarkably as response signal after mixing with DPA, while the emission intensity of CDs at 464 nm still remains unchanged as an internal reference (Fig. 3f). The change trends of zeta potential and hydrodynamic size for CDs-CAA/Eu3+ are similar with CAA/Eu3+ in the absence and presence of DPA (Fig. 3g-3h). The fluorescence intensity of single CDs, CDs+Eu3+, and CAA shows negligible change after introducing DPA (Fig. S16 and S11). Therefore, it is feasible to detect DPA by establishing a ratiometric fluorescence probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ based on ligand displacement sensing mechanism.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Parameters optimization for the detection of DPA
ce:section.ce:para: In order to achieve more excellent performance for the ratiometric determination of DPA, some important parameters were optimized including types and concentrations of metal ions (Al3+ and Eu3+), the ratios of CDs to CAA/Eu3+, and the reaction time. The introduction of Al3+ exceptionally improves the fluorescence of CuNCs due to AIE effect, while other metal cations show little effect ( Fig. 4a). The optimum concentration of Al3+ at 3.33 mM is chosen (Fig. 4b), and then adding Eu3+, the fluorescence CAA is remarkably quenched because of ACQ behavior. On the contrary, the ﬂuorescence intensity of CAA is almost unchanged in the presence of other metal ions (Fig. 4c). The optimized concentration of Eu3+ at 600 μM is chosen for the construction of CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite (Fig. 4d). In order to obtain better visualization performance, the volume ratio of CDs to CAA/Eu3+ (1:12) was selected

because of a bright fluorescent color change from blue to pink under UV lamp (Fig. 4e). The fluorescence intensity tends to be a platform when the incubation time is 10 min for CAA, 15 min for CAA/Eu3+, and 15 min for CAA/Eu3+ with DPA, respectively. Interestingly, the ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ immediately reaches a platform after adding DPA (Fig. 4f), indicating the ultra-rapid response compared to CAA/Eu3+ as a single response probe. To ensure the accurate results, the reaction time is set at 1.0 min. Finally, the optimal parameters are chosen: Al3+ at 3.33 mM, Eu3+ at 600 μM, the ratio of CDs to CAA/Eu3+ (1:12), reaction time of 10 min for CAA, 15 min for CAA/Eu3+, 15 min for CAA/Eu3+-DPA, and 1.0 min for CDs-CAA/Eu3+-DPA.

Under the optimized conditions, the emission intensity of a single-color ﬂuorescent probes of CAA/Eu3+ is gradually enhanced ( Fig. 5a), and its orange-red brightness is difficult to be distinguished by naked eyes with increasing DPA concentrations (Inset of Fig. 5a). CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite generate fluorescence responses with a linearity range from 6 µM to 80 μM and a LOD of 1.9 µM (Fig. 5b). Moreover, a ratiometric ﬂuorescent probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ nanocomposite is employed for the detection of DPA. The emission intensity of CAA at 610 nm is increased gradually with increasing the concentration of DPA, while the emission intensity of CDs at 464 nm has almost no variation (Fig. 5c). Simultaneously, the ratiometric probe presents a highly discriminative color change from blue to purple and even to bright pink under a 365 nm UV lamp irradiation (Inset of Fig. 5c). Meanwhile, the obvious variation of color can be distinguished easily with the naked eyes as confirmed by CIE chromaticity (Fig. 5d). A good linearity for CDs-CAA/Eu3+ probe is achieved between the ratio values (I464/I610) and the concentration of DPA in the range of 0.3 µM to 550 µM (Fig. 5e). LOD is estimated to be about 0.1 µM according to the 3σ/k rule, which is greatly lower than the minimum infectious dosage of anthrax spores (60 μM). Comparison with the previously reports in Table S1, our proposed method is comparable or better in terms of both LOD and detection range. As a result, the ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ offers the higher sensitivity, lower LOD, wider linear range, better visualization, and faster analysis than a single emission probe of CAA/Eu3+ for the determination of DPA.

Selectivity is a crucial parameter to evaluate a fluorescence sensor in the actual application. The possible interferential substances are abundantly present in many bacterial spores. The concentration of substance at 1.5 mM is three times that of DPA at 500 μM. Only DPA and coexisting species cause the significant ratiometric fluorescence changes, other interferential substances show negligible effects on the fluorescence of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ (Fig. 5f and Fig. S17). The excellent selectivity of a ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ may be ascribed to the oxygen atoms in the two carboxylate groups and nitrogen atoms in the aromatic ring of DPA with a clamped configuration, which helps to form stronger coordination with lanthanide ions and ion-pairing interaction than other interferents [46]. Notably, the ratiometric probe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ almost remains unchanged for 7 days (Fig. S18), indicating that it has good stability within a week.

Compared to spectroscopic detection technique, a portable, quick, and visual method for DPA quantitative assay is more important for on-site diagnosis. Recently, visual detection integrated with a smartphone has been undergoing rapid development because of their advantages of fast speed, practicability, and convenience [47,48]. In view of the excellent performance of CDs-CAA/Eu3+, we further integrate this ratiometric probe with a smartphone ( Fig. 6a). The different concentrations of DPA are added into the solution of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ and then put them in 96-well plate after full reaction for 1.0 min. A smartphone is applied to extract the red-green-blue (RGB) intensity of the obtained photographs under the 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 6b). A good linear relationship between the ratio of B/R and DPA concentration range from 8.0 to 400 μM can be obtained and LOD is estimated to be 5.7 μM. This is much more sensitive than the infectious dose (60 μM) spores. These results demonstrate that this method has a potential on-site application for visual and quantitative measure of DPA due to its simplicity and high sensitivity.

Since Bacillus anthracis is highly contagious for living organisms, we are unable to conduct real infectious sample testing. To further inspect the practicability of the proposed method, the DPA content in different real samples including soda water, orange juice, milk, bovine serum, and soil is determined by using the standard spiking method. The different concentrations of DPA (40, 250, and 500 μM) are added into in real samples as listed in Table 1. It can be seen that the recovery rates of DPA fall within the range of 97.21–102.25 % with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of less than 1.38 %. These results affirm that the proposed method can provide the stable and accurate detection efficacy in complex real samples.

In summary, a ratiometric ﬂuorescent nanoprobe of CDs-CAA/Eu3+ has been successfully established for the determination of DPA based on the ligand displacement sensing strategy. CAA nanocomposite originate from AIE behavior of CuNCs caused by Al3+, whose fluorescence intensity is about 280-fold higher than dispersed CuNCs. Notably, CAA emitting orange-red ﬂuorescence can be quenched by Eu3+ due to ACQ effect. CAA/Eu3+ is designed as the sensing response signals. Upon introducing DPA, the quenched orange-red ﬂuorescence of CAA is restored due to the higher chelation affinity between Eu3+ and DPA compared to CAA, leading to the detachment of Eu3+ from the surface of CAA and the formation of Eu3+-DPA complexes. Meanwhile, the blue fluorescence intensity of CDs as the internal reference almost remains constant during the whole process. Compared to a single emission probe of CAA/Eu3+, the ratiometric probe CDs-CAA/Eu3+ provides the higher sensitivity, lower LOD, wider linear range, better visualization, and faster analysis for the determination of DPA. Furthermore, the CDs-CAA/Eu3+ can be facilely combining with a portable smartphone, which can be easily realized the rapid visualization detection of DPA by the naked eyes. Therefore, this proposed method will provide broad application prospects in health diagnosis, environmental monitoring, food safety, and environment monitoring fields.

Huashuo Dou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Software, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yuexiang Lu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiwen Zhang: Visualization, Software, Methodology, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Jiaxi Wang: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Validation, Formal analysis. Yueying Liu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Key Project of Science and Technology Plan of Beijing Education Commission (Grant No. KZ20231002807) and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 21976104).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2025.138063.
